ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'tart' class; no specific cheese tart subclass exists. Mapped to the closest broader class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific cheese tart entry; mapped to the broader pastries and cakes category A0FKP.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for cheese tart; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cheese tart weighs approximately 60 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as sold in bakeries across Singapore and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: Cheese Tart — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cheese Tart (cr_id F11110005). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central cheese tart / cream cheese pastry analogues as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Pastry and Shortcrust-based Products — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (shortcrust pastry / custard tart entries)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for cheese tart exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous entries: custard tart (GI ~50–58, University of Sydney GI Database) and shortcrust pastry products (GI ~50–60). A midpoint of 55 is used. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (33.9 g/100g × 60 g serving = 20.34 g) ÷ 100 = 11.2 g GL. GI 55 falls into the 'low' category (GI < 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (14.7 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100g) triggers insulin-mediated sodium retention; estimated saturated fat (~13.5 g/100g) contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid regular consumption of cheese tart; the high sugar load (14.7 g/100g) with negligible fibre (1.0 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention independently of the moderate sodium content (140 mg/100g), and the high estimated saturated fat (~13.5 g/100g) further elevates cardiovascular risk — limit to very small portions on rare occasions only.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (33.9 g/100g) and sugar (14.7 g/100g) with minimal dietary fibre (1.0 g/100g) producing a low GI of 55 and glycemic load of 11.2 per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit cheese tart consumption; the combination of 33.9 g/100g refined carbohydrates, 14.7 g/100g sugar, and only 1.0 g/100g dietary fibre results in a low GI (55) and a glycemic load of 11.2 per 60 g tart, which can still cause meaningful postprandial glucose excursions — if consumed, restrict to one small tart (40–50 g) paired with a protein-rich food and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat (~13.5 g/100g) from cream cheese and butter far exceeds the 5 g/100g cardiovascular threshold, combined with high sugar (14.7 g/100g) and low fibre (1.0 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular cheese tart consumption; the estimated saturated fat content of approximately 13.5 g/100g (from cream cheese and butter) substantially exceeds cardiovascular dietary thresholds, and the high sugar content (14.7 g/100g) with low fibre (1.0 g/100g) further elevates LDL cholesterol and triglyceride risk — treat as an occasional indulgence only and limit portion to one small tart.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat (~13.5 g/100g) far exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; high sugar (14.7 g/100g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid cheese tart; the estimated saturated fat (~13.5 g/100g from cream cheese and butter) triggers TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine pathways, and the high sugar content (14.7 g/100g) independently promotes systemic inflammation — no anti-inflammatory components are present to offset these effects.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese tart is a baked pastry consisting of a buttery shortcrust or pâte sucrée shell filled with a rich, creamy cheese custard made from cream cheese, eggs, sugar, and milk. The tart shell provides a crumbly, golden-brown exterior with a slightly crisp bite, while the filling is smooth, dense, and mildly tangy with a sweet, dairy-forward flavour. Cheese tarts became widely popular across Singapore and Malaysia through Japanese-inspired bakery chains and are now a mainstream snack and dessert item sold in shopping malls and bakeries throughout the region. Per 100 g, cheese tart delivers 371 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 23.23 g fat (estimated saturated fat ~13.5 g), and 33.9 g carbohydrates including 14.7 g sugar and only 1.0 g dietary fibre. The sodium content is moderate at 140 mg/100g. Clinically, cheese tart is a high-concern food for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to its high saturated fat, high sugar, and low fibre profile. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low) with a glycemic load of approximately 11.2 per standard 60 g serving. Calcium content is 71 mg/100g, contributing modestly to daily intake. This food is vegetarian and generally considered halal, but contains gluten, dairy, and eggs as major allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a cheese tart?
Is cheese tart suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of cheese tart?
How much fat is in a cheese tart?
What allergens are in cheese tart?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11110005) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central cream cheese pastry analogues and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous custard tart and shortcrust pastry entries with documented basis. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible for this food type.
notes: Saturated fat (13.5 g/100g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central cream cheese pastry and shortcrust tart analogues, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; this value should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this item and no sufficiently close approved-source analogue provides reliable estimates for all of these fields. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database custard tart and shortcrust pastry entries (GI range 50–60); a direct measurement for cheese tart is not available in any approved GI source as of 2026-04-16. Glycemic load per serving = 55 × (33.9 × 0.60) / 100 = 11.2. Halal status assumed based on typical commercial preparation in Singapore/Malaysia; individual products should be verified against MUIS or equivalent certification.
